scenario: New users confused which URL to use. Wonders Operate vs Tasklist endpoints.
question: Operate vs Tasklist URLs?

options.text: Different services / URLs: - Operate: admin / operator view; shows process instances, incidents, decision instances; allows cancel, retry, variable modification. io/<cluster-id> (SaaS) or configured path prefix for Self-Managed. - Tasklist: end-user view; shows User Tasks assigned to or claimable by the logged-in user; form rendering and task completion. io/<cluster-id> (SaaS) or separate path for Self-Managed. - Different audiences: operators and developers use Operate; business users and assignees use Tasklist. - Different APIs: Operate REST API for querying instances; Tasklist REST API for task management. Both secured with the same OAuth client credentials in SaaS.

Operate and Tasklist share the same URL and application -- this does not accurately reflect the behavior. Operate and Tasklist are separate web applications deployed as distinct services with separate URL paths or subdomains. io/<cluster-id>. In local development and Self-Managed deployments, they typically run on different path prefixes or ports. They serve different user roles and have different purposes, UIs, and API surfaces.

Only Operate is available; Tasklist functionality is included within Operate -- this does not accurately reflect the behavior. Tasklist is a distinct UI application specifically designed for end users to view, claim, and complete User Tasks assigned to them. It has a task-focused interface with form rendering, task assignment, and completion workflows. Operate serves operators and developers who need to monitor process instances, investigate incidents, cancel instances, and retry jobs. These are fundamentally different audiences and use cases that warrant separate dedicated applications.

Operate and Tasklist are a single combined service that serves all purposes -- this does not accurately reflect the behavior. They are separate components in the Camunda 8 architecture with distinct responsibilities. Operate is the operational monitoring and control UI, providing visibility into process instances, incidents, and the ability to take administrative actions. Tasklist is the human task management UI for business users completing assigned work items. Running them as separate services allows them to be scaled, secured, and maintained independently according to different load profiles and access control requirements.

a.text: Correct. Operate vs Tasklist: Operate: For: ops / admin / developers. Shows: process instances, incidents, decision instances. Actions: cancel instance, modify variables, retry incidents. URL format (SaaS): https://.operate.camunda.io/. Self-Managed: Configurable; e.g., https://operate.example.com. Tasklist: For: end users / task assignees. Shows: User Tasks to complete. Actions: claim, complete, save draft. URL format (SaaS): https://.tasklist.camunda.io/. Self-Managed: Configurable. Web Modeler: For: BPMN/DMN/Form authors. URL: separate service. Camunda Console: For: org admins. Manages: clusters, users, billing. All under same domain typically: SaaS: camunda.io subdomains. Self-Managed: per setup. For SaaS: Camunda Console: links to Operate, Tasklist per cluster. Single sign-on: across services. One user: multiple services. For Self-Managed: Configure URLs: per deployment. Reverse proxy: typically. TLS: certs for each subdomain. For authentication: Same Identity / Keycloak: shared. Per-service permissions. For navigation: From Console: link to services. From Operate: link to Tasklist for related task. Cross-service navigation: per Camunda 8 version. For URL structure (typical): ` https://.operate.camunda.io//processes https://.tasklist.camunda.io//tasks https://.modeler.cloud.camunda.io/ Cluster ID + path. For Web Modeler: Org-level: not per cluster. Single Web Modeler instance: per org. For API endpoints (Pattern #11 -- 8.8 canonical is Orchestration Cluster REST API on single host:port): Orchestration Cluster REST API: unified entry per apis-tools/orchestration-cluster-api-rest/orchestration-cluster-api-rest-overview/ -- covers what was previously split across Zeebe / Operate / Tasklist v1 APIs. Legacy Operate v1 / Tasklist v1 paths: still available for backward compatibility but new integrations should target the Orchestration Cluster REST API. Zeebe gRPC: still the high-throughput worker transport. For SDK clients: Each service: separate base URL. Configure per: in SDK. For deep links: Direct to instance: operate.../processes/. Direct to task: tasklist.../tasks/`. Useful for notifications. For embedding: Iframe Tasklist: in own UI. Verify CORS: configure. For mobile: Tasklist mobile UX: responsive. Or: custom mobile app. For multi-cluster: One Operate / Tasklist per cluster. Navigate between clusters: in Console. For SSO: Log in once: access all services. Per Set 14 Q54. For Operate use cases: Investigate stuck process. Retry incident. Cancel instance. View decision instance. For Tasklist use cases: Claim available task. Complete task with form. View my tasks. Comment / collaboration. For audit / compliance: Per-service audit logs. Operate operations: changes tracked. Tasklist completions: tracked. For role separation: Operator role: Operate. End user role: Tasklist. Different permissions. For multi-language: Both Tasklist + Operate: localised. User locale settings. For accessibility: WCAG compliance: both. Screen readers, etc. For new users orientation: Document: which service for what. Onboarding guides: link to specific. Role-based: only relevant services. For confusion prevention: Bookmarks: per user role. Clear UI navigation: link from Console. For testing: Each service: smoke test. Auth flow: SSO works. Anti-patterns: One URL for everything: wrong mental model. Shared sessions across services: handled by SSO. Hardcoded URLs: configure-able. Best practices: Document per role: which service. SSO: simplify access. Bookmarks: user efficiency. For the scenario (URL confusion): Operate for ops: incidents, etc. Tasklist for tasks: complete work. Different audiences, different URLs. For Self-Managed deployment: Configure URLs: per env. Domain names: company-internal. TLS: required for prod. For DNS: Subdomains: per service. Wildcard cert: covers all. For load balancing: Per service: own load balancer. High availability.
